title: Copenhagen & Denmark
dek: Nine days around one of Europe's most liveable cities — canal cruises and rooftop ski slopes, a bridge to Sweden, an overnight ferry to Norway, and three days at Pokémon GO Fest. Tick items off as you go; your progress saves automatically.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Copenhagen base at CABINN by Nørreport, with day trips to Malmö and an overnight sailing to Oslo.

sections.title: Before you leave — final checks
sections.body: Give these a last look in the week before you fly. They're the things most likely to have shifted since this guide was written.
checklist: Go Nordic Cruiseline Copenhagen→Oslo sailing time & terminal — re-check at gonordiccruiseline.com
Vækst dinner, Sat Jun 13 17:30 — confirm the reservation still stands
Opening hours for any sight you've built a day around
Copenhagen forecast Jun 8–16 — pack layers and a waterproof
GO Fest park ticket session time matches what you bought
Passport valid, cards set for abroad, Verizon plan confirmed active

sections.title: Off the plane — what to do first
sections.body: At the airport, in this order:
checklist: Clear EES biometrics — photo and fingerprints; this is the EU's new entry system, your first time through it
Open the DOT Tickets app and buy a City Pass Small before you board the metro
Take Metro M2 from Lufthavnen to Nørreport (~15–20 min) — City Pass covers the ride
Walk ~5 min to CABINN and check in
Confirm Verizon is active — if not on Unlimited Ultimate, text TRAVEL to 4004 to enable TravelPass

sections.title: The basics — what every first-timer should know
sections.body: Emergency: 112. Tap water is safe and delicious everywhere in Denmark — carry a refillable bottle. Tipping is not expected or required; rounding up at a restaurant is plenty. Rejseplanen is the official transit app — type in any two addresses and it gives you door-to-door directions. Two phrases go a long way: tak (thanks) and hej (hi, or bye).

sections.title: Entry & what to carry
sections.body: ⚠️ This assumes a US passport. Different passport? Check your own requirements — they can differ significantly from the US.
checklist: Passport valid at least 3 months beyond Jun 16
No ETIAS needed yet — the EU's pre-travel authorisation has been delayed past 2026
Expect EES biometric capture at your first EU entry point (Copenhagen airport)
Download or print confirmations: Go Nordic Cruiseline ferry + cabin, GO Fest tickets + Premier Access, Vækst dinner (Sat Jun 13 at 17:30)
A Type E/F EU plug adapter — one power strip covers the whole group from a single outlet
A proper rain layer — Copenhagen's weather changes fast in June

sections.title: Everything you need to book, in order
sections.body: Start at the top — these are the things that sell out or set your dates for everything else.
checklist: Flights, round-trip — ≈ $400–1,200 depending on dates and timing
Go Nordic Cruiseline Copenhagen–Oslo ferry + a cabin — ≈ $220 return; books up early, especially in summer
Hotel — CABINN by Nørreport — ≈ $110/night; book early for June
GO Fest park ticket (200 kr ≈ $31) + Premier Access add-on (640 kr ≈ $99) — match the session to your day
Mom's group: GetYourGuide canal tour + hop-on-hop-off bus for Thu and Fri — book a few days ahead
Vækst dinner, Sat Jun 13 17:30 — popular restaurant, reserve early
Travel insurance (a few weeks out)
Confirm which Verizon plan you're on — see Phone & data section (a few weeks out)
Passport valid check — at least 3 months beyond Jun 16 (check about a month out)
Download Rejseplanen + offline maps for Copenhagen, Malmö, and Oslo (the week before)
Download DOT Tickets app; buy City Pass on arrival — see current price at dinoffentligetransport.dk
Break in your shoes and insoles — you'll be walking 12,000+ steps a day

sections.title: Phone & data — Denmark, Sweden & Norway
sections.body:
Which Verizon plan you're on changes everything here. Check before you leave.

On Unlimited Ultimate: International data, calls and texts are already included — no daily charge, and you can't add TravelPass to this plan even if you wanted to. You get 15 GB of high-speed roaming data per month, then unlimited at 1.5 Mbps for the rest of your billing cycle. All three countries on this trip — Denmark, Sweden (Malmö) and Norway (Oslo) — draw from the same monthly pool.

On any other Unlimited plan (Plus, Welcome, etc.): Use TravelPass at $12/line/day. It activates automatically the first time you use data, make a call, or send a text in a given country — you're only charged on days you actually use it. You get 5 GB high-speed then unlimited at 3G speeds per 24-hour window. Text TRAVEL to 4004 to pre-enable it before you fly. Each country counts separately when you cross a border.

Both plans: No phone signal in the middle of the North Sea on the overnight ferry. Download offline maps, the Go Nordic Cruiseline app, and anything else you'll want before you board on Jun 14.

sections.title: Footwear & comfort
sections.body: You'll be on your feet 6–10 hours most days. For heavier build or heel pain, choose a firm insole with a deep heel cup — not a soft gel pad, which compresses and stops helping within an hour. Good options: Powerstep Pinnacle Maxx, Tread Labs Pace, Superfeet Green, or heavy-duty brands like VALSOLE / Walkomfy (rated 220+ lb). Buy them a week before the trip and break them in.

sections.title: GoPro settings cheat-sheet
sections.body: General footage: 4K/30 or 2.7K/60 with HyperSmooth on. Slow-mo: 1080/120. Pack two fully charged spare batteries — the cold harbour wind drains them faster than at home. Use a fast 128 GB+ card and format it before you leave.

sections.title: When is a good time to go — and what to know about June
sections.body:
Good news: no Danish public holidays fall during Jun 8–16, 2026. The spring bank holidays (Ascension, Whit Monday, and Constitution Day on Jun 5) all land just before you arrive, and Midsummer Eve (Jun 23) is just after. Shops, museums, and transit run on normal weekday and weekend schedules throughout your stay.

What to plan around: you're travelling in peak summer, which means roughly 17–18 hours of daylight each day, maximum crowds at popular spots, and the highest prices of the year. And Pokémon GO Fest fills Fælledparken Jun 12–14, drawing a large international crowd that packs central hotels, restaurants, and transit on those days. Most Copenhagen shops open seven days a week in summer; smaller neighbourhood shops may shorten Sunday hours.

sections.title: Currency & how to pay
sections.body:
Denmark uses the krone (DKK) — not the euro. Rough mid-2026 guide rate (verify before you go): 100 DKK ≈ $15.50 USD. Your day trips use different currencies: 100 SEK ≈ $10–11 (Sweden) and 100 NOK ≈ $9–10 (Norway).

Cards and contactless payments work essentially everywhere in Denmark — you may never need to touch cash. Tipping is not part of Danish culture; rounding up at a restaurant for genuinely good service is plenty, and no one expects it.

sections.title: What this trip costs — a realistic budget
sections.intro: Rough per-person estimates in USD. Denmark is consistently one of Europe's most expensive destinations — these numbers are honest starting points, not minimums. Tap any 'Your spend' field to log what you actually pay; the total updates live and is saved on this device.

items.label: Lodging — CABINN, per night (per room)
items.note: Split across the room if sharing.

items.label: Food & coffee, per day
items.note: Restaurant mains ≈$25–48; bakeries and street food are much cheaper. Torvehallerne lunch ≈$12–20.

items.label: Local transport — City Pass Small (100 DKK/day)
items.note: 100 DKK ≈ $15.50. Covers metro, bus, S-tog and harbour buses in zones 1–4 (including the airport). Check current price at dinoffentligetransport.dk.

items.label: Flights — round trip
items.note: NYC↔CPH runs roughly $400–1,200 by date and timing.

items.label: Go Nordic Cruiseline ferry — return cabin
items.note: Foot-passenger with inside cabin. Prices vary — book early at gonordiccruiseline.com.

items.label: Malmö day trip — Øresundståg return
items.note: ≈111 DKK each way from Nørreport. Buy at the station — City Pass doesn't cover the Swedish leg.

items.label: GO Fest park ticket (200 kr per session)
items.note: 200 kr ≈ $31. Morning (9:00–13:00) or Afternoon (14:00–18:00) session. Includes full citywide access that day.

items.label: GO Fest Premier Access (optional add-on)
items.note: 640 kr ≈ $99. Exclusive collectible + voucher codes for 100+ in-game items. Set to 0 if skipping.

items.label: Mom's group — canal tour + hop-on-hop-off (per person, 2 days)
items.note: ≈ Stromma canal tour ~129 DKK + RED Sightseeing bus 24h ~199–299 DKK. Check current prices on GetYourGuide before booking.

items.label: Copenhagen Card option (instead of daily transit + entries)
items.note: 589 / 859 / 1,039 DKK for 24h / 48h / 72h (verified Jun 2026 at copenhagencard.com). Covers all transit zones + 80+ attractions free. Use INSTEAD of the transport and sights daily lines — not on top of them.

sections.title: Getting medical help in Copenhagen
sections.body:
Emergency — dial 112 (free from any phone): ambulance, fire, police. Non-emergency medical line: 1813 — Copenhagen's 24/7 nurse and doctor advice line. Call this before going to any emergency room; they'll direct you. From a foreign mobile: +45 1813. Non-emergency police: 114.

Pharmacies (apotek) are marked with a green 'A'. The only one open around the clock is Steno Apotek, Vesterbrogade 6C — directly opposite Central Station. English-speaking staff; after 23:00, use the night bell on the right side of the entrance. As a US visitor you have no Danish health insurance card, but 1813 can still advise you; private English-speaking GP and hotel-visit services are available for a fee. Tap water is safe throughout Denmark.
checklist: Save 112 (emergency) and +45 1813 (medical advice) in your phone now
Pack all medicines in original packaging with a copy of any prescriptions
Confirm travel insurance covers overseas medical care
24-hour pharmacy: Steno Apotek, Vesterbrogade 6C

sections.title: How to fit in — Danish etiquette for visitors
sections.body:
Danish is the official language, but virtually everyone under 60 speaks fluent English — you will not struggle. A few local habits worth knowing before you arrive:

The bike lane is sacred. Copenhagen runs on cycling. The painted lane between the pavement and the road belongs exclusively to cyclists — do not step into it, stop in it for photos, or cut across it without looking. Cyclists move fast and expect the lane clear. Look both ways before crossing.

Cards, not cash. Denmark is one of the world's most cashless societies. Contactless payments work in taxis, market stalls, museums, and corner shops. You can carry virtually no cash and be fine.

Tipping is not expected. Service charges are included in all restaurant bills by law. Rounding up for exceptional service is perfectly fine; no one will be offended if you don't. 'Tak' (thanks) and 'hej' (hi or bye) go a long way.

sections.title: How to get everywhere on this trip
steps: Airport → hotel: Metro M2 from CPH airport (Lufthavnen station) to Nørreport (~15–20 min). Buy a City Pass Small in the DOT Tickets app before boarding — it covers the airport ride (zones 1–4). Walk ~5 min to CABINN.

Around Copenhagen: Harbour buses 991 and 992 for waterfront hops (City Pass covers them). The driverless metro for cross-city trips. Donkey Republic e-bikes for short, flat stretches if you want a break from walking.

To Malmö (Jun 10): Øresundståg from Nørreport or København H to Malmö C (~35–40 min). Bring your passport — there are ID checks on the bridge crossing. Buy a separate cross-border ticket at the station; your City Pass stops at the Danish border.
To the Oslo ferry (Jun 14): Taxi or shuttle to Go Nordic Cruiseline terminal, Dampfærgevej 30 (Østerbro). Check in by 16:15; sailing departs 16:30.
Oslo ashore (Jun 15): Dock at Vippetangen ~10:00. Walk 10–15 min to the Oslo Opera House roof, Aker Brygge, and Akershus Fortress loop. Reboard by 15:30.
Dock → airport (Jun 16): Back in Copenhagen ~10:00. Take a taxi directly to CPH (~20 min) — safest option for the 14:20 flight.

sections.title: Transit passes — which one to buy and when
sections.body:
Three real options for this trip, from simplest to most comprehensive:

City Pass Small — the default choice, bought in the DOT Tickets app. Covers all metro lines (M1–M4), S-tog commuter rail, local buses, and harbour buses 991/992. Zones 1–4, which includes the CPH airport. Verified 2026 prices: 24h = 100 DKK, 48h = 160 DKK, 96h = 280 DKK, 120h = 340 DKK. Note: there is no 72-hour option — the jump is from 48h to 96h.

Copenhagen Card (DISCOVER) — covers unlimited transit in all zones (including airport) plus free admission to 80+ attractions, including Rosenborg Castle, the Round Tower, and the National Museum. Verified 2026 prices: 24h = 589 DKK, 48h = 859 DKK, 72h = 1,039 DKK. Worth doing the maths before you buy — it only makes sense on days you're visiting 3+ paid attractions. Full pricing at copenhagencard.com.

Single tickets — fine for one-off journeys. A standard 2-zone city ticket is ≈26 DKK; the airport is a 3-zone journey if you're buying a single (~36–40 DKK, but a City Pass Small covers it). Buy at station machines or in the DOT app.

What City Pass does NOT cover: the Øresundståg to Malmö, the Go Nordic Cruiseline ferry, or any travel in Sweden or Norway.
checklist: Download the DOT Tickets app before you leave home
Buy City Pass Small on arrival at the airport — 100 DKK/24h (verified 2026 price)
Malmö day (Jun 10): buy a separate Øresundståg cross-border ticket at Nørreport station
Museum-heavy days: check if Copenhagen Card (589+ DKK) saves money vs City Pass + individual entries

sections.title: Step-by-step routes — Thursday, Friday & Saturday
steps: Thu — GO Fest group · LEGO → CopenHill → Superkilen → Grundtvig's: (1) Walk from CABINN south via Nørregade → Strøget to LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet 37 (~15 min, ≈1 km). Browse freely — the Stamp Rally doesn't open until tomorrow. (2) Metro M1 south to DR Byen (4 stops, ~8 min) then bus 2A or walk ≈600 m to CopenHill; or taxi from Rådhuspladsen (~10 min, ≈100–140 DKK). (3) Return to city: bus 5C from Nørreport north to Nørrebrogade for Superkilen (~15 min). (4) Bus 6A from Nørrebro to Bispebjerg for Grundtvig's Church (~20 min). ⚠️ Grundtvig's closes at 15:45 — plan to arrive no later than 15:15. Return on bus 6A or 6C to Nørreport.

Thu — Mom's group · Canal boat → hop-on-hop-off bus: Metro M1/M2 to Kongens Nytorv (~5 min from CABINN) then walk ~5 min to Nyhavn or Gammel Strand for the canal tour — confirm exact departure pier from your GetYourGuide booking. After the cruise (~1 hr), join the RED Sightseeing hop-on-hop-off bus at the nearest stop (Kongens Nytorv, Nyhavn, or Rådhuspladsen). Ride the full loop once to orient, then hop off at leisure.

Fri AM — GO Fest group · CABINN → Fælledparken (9:00 park session): Walk east: Nørre Allé → Blegdamsvej → Fælledparken south entrance (~20–25 min, ≈1.8 km, flat). OR Metro M2 one stop to Østerport (~2 min) then walk south ~10 min to the park's east gate at Øster Allé. Aim to arrive by 8:45 to clear the entry queue.

Fri PM — GO Fest group · Park exit → lunch → Stamp Rally begins: Session ends 13:00. Walk west (~20 min) and stop at Torvehallerne for lunch — the market exits directly onto Nørreport station. Then walk ~15 min south down Strøget to LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet (open until 18:00 today) for your first Stamp Rally stamps. Evening: LEGO Store Tivoli Gardens is open until 22:00 if you want more stamps — no rush.

Fri — Mom's group · Hop-on-hop-off bus day: Board at Kongens Nytorv or Rådhuspladsen. Suggested stops: Nyhavn (waterfront, canal views) → Rosenborg Castle and the King's Garden → Frederiksberg palace park. All on the bus pass; no separate tickets needed for transport.

Sat — GO Fest group · Citywide play → leave by 16:30: Best PokéStop clusters: Strøget/Vimmelskaftet (LEGO Store, Royal Copenhagen, central stops) and the Nyhavn–Kongens Nytorv loop (waterfront, dense stops). For the Stamp Rally: LEGO Vimmelskaftet is open 10:00–18:00, LEGO Tivoli Gardens 10:00–22:00. Collect your remaining stamps for the 5-stamp Pikachu encounter. Hard stop at 16:30 — leave wherever you are.

Sat — Mom's group · Tivoli → Vækst: Tivoli Gardens, Vesterbrogade 3 — 10-min walk or short metro from CABINN. Buy tickets at tivoli.dk in advance to skip the queue (dynamic pricing, from ≈150 DKK in summer; verify before going). Leave Tivoli by 17:00 — taxi to Vækst is ~10–15 min. All five people at Vækst at 17:30.

items.title: First steps in Copenhagen
items.pace: Arrival half-day · airport → hotel ~20 min · easy harbour-bus loop ~1–2 hr
items.note: Keep day one light. You've just crossed the Atlantic — your body doesn't know what time it is.
items.body:
A gentle landing. Find your bearings, put the luggage down, and let the harbour do the heavy lifting.

Land, clear EES biometrics, ride the M2 to Nørreport, and check in at CABINN. Then an easy harbour-bus loop along the waterfront — your City Pass covers it. Nyhavn looks best in the evening light, and this is a low-pressure way to see the city at your own pace without a plan.
checklist: City Pass Small active in the DOT Tickets app
Refill a water bottle — tap water here is excellent
Dinner somewhere close; early night recommended to beat the jet lag

items.title: Fortresses, mermaids & the best street food market in Scandinavia
items.pace: ~5–6 hr out · Kastellet + Little Mermaid ~1 hr · harbour bus to Reffen ~20 min · Reffen ~1.5–2 hr
items.note: Lots of walking in the morning — pace it. There's plenty of seating at Reffen once you arrive.
items.body:
Copenhagen at its most ancient and its most now — a 400-year-old star fort before noon, 60 international kitchens for lunch.

Morning at Kastellet, one of the best-preserved star-shaped fortresses in northern Europe with scenic ramparts to walk. A short detour takes you to the Little Mermaid — lovely, though smaller than photographs suggest. Afterwards, board a harbour bus out to Reffen for lunch among the food stalls. The market opens at 11:30 and runs until 21:30; it's the largest street food market in the Nordics.
items.fit: Layers — the waterfront is breezy in any weather.
checklist: Card or small cash for food stalls
Camera / GoPro charged

items.title: Across the bridge — a half-day in Sweden
items.pace: Full day · train ~35 min each way · Gamla Staden + Turning Torso ~4–5 hr
items.note: You need your passport — there are ID checks on the Øresund bridge crossing. No exceptions.
items.body:
Just 35 minutes by train, but Malmö feels like a different world — cobbled medieval squares and a tower that corkscrews 190 metres into the sky.

Take the Øresundståg from Nørreport across the Øresund Bridge to Malmö C. Wander Gamla Staden (the old town) and Lilla Torg, then out to the waterfront for the twisting Turning Torso. Buy your cross-border ticket at Nørreport station before boarding — City Pass covers Copenhagen only, and does not work in Sweden.
items.fit: Same as a city day; comfortable shoes.
checklist: Passport (mandatory for ID checks at the border)
Buy a separate cross-border Øresundståg ticket at Nørreport station
Check the return train timetable before you leave Copenhagen

items.title: Two crews, two cities — a rooftop ski slope, the world's most famous brick store & a guided cruise
items.pace: Full day · two separate plans · meet up for dinner · see 'Day routes' for step-by-step paths
items.note: The LEGO Stamp Rally doesn't open until Friday — Thursday is a pure city day for the GO Fest group. ⚠️ Grundtvig's Church closes at 15:45; plan to arrive by 15:15 at the latest.
items.body:
The group splits for the day. The Pokémon crew plots a vertical loop across Copenhagen. The mums float through the canals, then see the city from the top of a double-decker.

🎮 GO Fest group (you + +1): Start at the LEGO flagship on Strøget (Vimmelskaftet 37) — browse and pick up anything you want; come back for Stamp Rally tomorrow. Then CopenHill (Amager Bakke) for rooftop skyline-and-sea views — free to visit, accessible by lift. Next, the striped Superkilen plaza in Nørrebro, and finally the extraordinary Grundtvig's Church in Bispebjerg — a cathedral of yellow brick that looks like a giant pipe organ inside. Close attention to timing: it closes at 15:45. Full step-by-step route in 'Day routes' above.

👵 Mom's group (3 people): Two tours booked ahead on GetYourGuide: (1) a 1-hour canal/harbour boat tour from Gammel Strand or Nyhavn — narrated cruise past Christianshavn, the opera house and the coloured canal houses (Stromma classic tour ≈129 DKK; budget option: Netto-Bådene ≈60 DKK); (2) a RED Sightseeing hop-on-hop-off bus (24h, ≈199–299 DKK — check before booking) covering Nyhavn, Amalienborg, Rosenborg, and Frederiksberg at their own pace. Mostly seated; low walking demand all day.
items.fit: GO Fest group: comfortable shoes and a layer for CopenHill's rooftop wind. Mom's group: light shoes — mostly seated.
checklist: GO Fest group: leave LEGO by ~10:30 to have time for all four stops before Grundtvig's closes
GO Fest group: GoPro charged + battery pack
Mom's group: GetYourGuide canal tour slot confirmed and pier noted
Mom's group: RED Sightseeing bus pass in hand or downloaded
All groups: agree on dinner location and time before splitting up in the morning

items.title: The gates open — GO Fest park morning, the Stamp Rally begins
items.pace: Split groups all day · GO Fest group: park 9:00–13:00 then Stamp Rally · Mom's group: hop-on-hop-off bus day
items.note: The LEGO Stamp Rally opens today. LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet closes at 18:00 — go this afternoon, don't leave Stamp Rally entirely to Saturday.
items.body:
The biggest morning of the trip. Into the park at sunrise-adjacent. Out into the city, stamps in hand, by afternoon.

🎮 GO Fest group (you + +1): Morning park session at Fælledparken (9:00–13:00, park ticket required). Walk from CABINN (~20–25 min) or Metro M2 to Østerport then 10 min south — full directions in 'Day routes' above. Arrive by 8:45 to beat the entry queue. After the session, lunch at Torvehallerne on the walk back (directly at Nørreport). Afternoon: straight to LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet (open until 18:00) to start the Stamp Rally. Evening: LEGO Store Tivoli Gardens (open until 22:00) for additional stamps if needed.

👵 Mom's group (3 people): Their full hop-on-hop-off bus day on RED Sightseeing. Board at Kongens Nytorv or Rådhuspladsen; ride the full loop once to orient, then hop off at Nyhavn for waterfront time, Rosenborg Castle and the King's Garden, and Frederiksberg palace park. Flexible and low-demand throughout. Meet for dinner as agreed.
items.fit: GO Fest group: hat, sunscreen, water bottle, battery pack — it will be warm in the park. Mom's group: comfortable shoes for on/off the bus.
checklist: GO Fest group: park ticket matches Morning session (9:00–13:00)
GO Fest group: Premier Access add-on loaded in the app (if purchased)
GO Fest group: battery pack + charging cable + water + sunscreen
GO Fest group: Torvehallerne for lunch right after exiting the park
GO Fest group: LEGO Vimmelskaftet this afternoon — it closes at 18:00
Mom's group: RED Sightseeing bus pass confirmed
All groups: agree on dinner time and place

items.title: Last stamps, full city — then the best dinner of the trip
items.pace: Split groups AM/PM · GO Fest group: hard stop 16:30 · all five at Vækst 17:30
items.note: Stamp Rally wraps up today — you need 5 total across both LEGO stores; you started yesterday, so you may only need a few more. Everyone at Vækst at 17:30 no matter what.
items.body:
One last day to hunt the city before all five meet at the table.

🎮 GO Fest group (you + +1): Citywide GO Fest gameplay all day. Best PokéStop clusters: Strøget/Vimmelskaftet (LEGO Store, Royal Copenhagen, central stops) and the Nyhavn–Kongens Nytorv loop. Stamp Rally: LEGO Vimmelskaftet (10:00–18:00) and LEGO Tivoli Gardens (10:00–22:00). Collect 5 stamps total → Pikachu with Special Background encounter. Hard stop at 16:30 — leave wherever you are.

👵 Mom's group (3 people): Tivoli Gardens (Vesterbrogade 3, 10-min walk from CABINN). Buy tickets at tivoli.dk in advance to skip the queue — entry from ≈150 DKK in summer (dynamic pricing; verify before going). Alternatively, Rosenborg Castle if they missed it Friday (~160 DKK entry, or free with Copenhagen Card). Leave by 17:00 for a taxi to Vækst (~10–15 min). All five meet at Vækst at 17:30.
items.fit: GO Fest group: walking shoes for a full pavement day. Mom's group: comfortable shoes for Tivoli. Everyone: something a step up from trainers for Vækst dinner.
checklist: Vækst reservation confirmed — 17:30, all 5 people
GO Fest group: hard stop 16:30, drop everything and head to Vækst
GO Fest group: LEGO Stamp Rally — 5 stamps total for the Pikachu encounter
Mom's group: Tivoli tickets bought in advance at tivoli.dk
Mom's group: leave Tivoli by 17:00
Agree on a meeting point near Vækst in case groups are in different parts of the city at 16:30

items.title: Last morning ashore — then overnight to Oslo
items.pace: Morning free · Torvehallerne ~1 hr · terminal check-in by 16:15, sailing 16:30
items.note: Don't cut the boarding window fine with luggage. The check-in deadline is 16:15.
items.body:
A slow last morning at the food market. Then the ferry terminal, the cabin, and the North Sea between you and Oslo.

Ease into the day at Torvehallerne for a final Copenhagen breakfast. Then take a taxi to the Go Nordic Cruiseline terminal at Dampfærgevej 30. Check in by 16:15 for the 16:30 sailing, find your cabin, and settle in for the overnight crossing.
checklist: Go Nordic Cruiseline ferry + cabin booking confirmation
Snacks and anything you'll want for the overnight crossing
Download offline maps, playlists, and the Go Nordic Cruiseline app — there's no phone signal in the middle of the North Sea

items.title: Six hours in Oslo — marble rooftops, a Viking waterfront & a medieval fortress
items.pace: ~6 hr ashore · dock ~10:00 · Opera + Aker Brygge + Akershus ~5 hr · reboard by 15:30
items.note: Mathallen food hall is closed on Mondays. Aker Brygge has plenty of good food options on the waterfront instead.
items.body:
Dock, see the city, reboard. Oslo gives you exactly one day — make it count.

Dock at Vippetangen around 10:00. Walk the white marble roof of the Oslo Opera House (free and open to all), stroll the waterfront restaurants and galleries of Aker Brygge, then visit Akershus Fortress — a medieval castle with harbour views that has stood since the 1290s. Reboard the ship by 15:30 for the return crossing.
items.fit: Layers — Oslo can be noticeably cooler than Copenhagen.
checklist: Watch the reboarding time — 15:30, not a minute later
Norwegian krone or card (Vipps mobile pay is widely used in Norway)

items.title: Ferry home, one last coffee, catch the flight
items.pace: Tight connection · dock ~10:00 → taxi ~20 min → CPH for 14:20
items.note: Go straight to the airport. This is not a day for one more detour.
items.body:
The ship docks at ten. The plane leaves at 14:20. Go straight to the airport.

Back in Copenhagen around 10:00. Take a taxi directly to CPH (~20 min) — this is the safe call for a tight connection. Handle any VAT refund at the airport before the flight.
checklist: Passport
VAT refund forms if you bought anything over 300 DKK at a participating store
Taxi to the airport — don't try to take the metro with heavy bags on a tight schedule

items.name: Nyhavn
items.body: The candy-coloured canal houses everyone photographs. Beautiful in any light — come early morning to beat the crowds, or glide past on the harbour bus to avoid them entirely.

items.name: Reffen
items.body: 60+ international stalls on the old shipyard at Refshaleøen, with picnic benches, firepits, and harbour views. Food stalls open 11:30–21:30 daily; bars until midnight Thu and 1 am Fri–Sat. reffen.dk

items.name: Kastellet
items.body: One of the best-preserved star-shaped fortresses in northern Europe — still an active military area, but the ramparts and grounds are open to walk freely. The Little Mermaid is a short detour away: lovely, but smaller than expected.

items.name: CopenHill (Amager Bakke)
items.kicker: A ski slope on top of a power plant
items.body: A waste-to-energy plant whose roof doubles as a year-round ski slope and hiking trail, with a free lift to the summit for views over the harbour and city. One of the most original pieces of architecture in Europe.

items.name: Superkilen
items.body: A surreal striped public park in Nørrebro that brings together objects collected from 60 different countries. Flat, photogenic, and genuinely local — not a tourist set-piece.

items.name: Grundtvig's Church
items.body: A massive yellow-brick 'organ-pipe' church in Bispebjerg — soaring, quiet, and genuinely unforgettable inside. Closed Mondays; closes at 15:45 every other day. Plan your Thursday route accordingly. grundtvigskirke.dk

sections.title: What to skip (or lower your expectations for)
sections.body: A few Copenhagen classics that over-promise. The Little Mermaid is worth a two-minute look — it's roughly the size of a labrador, not the towering figure the photos imply. Catch it while walking past Kastellet; don't plan a trip around it. Strøget's middle section is mostly the same international chains you have at home — the side streets and the ends of the street are far more interesting. And the GO Fest group doesn't need a paid boat tour — the harbour buses on your City Pass cover much of the same waterfront view for free. (Mom's group is in a different situation — their guided narrated cruise is exactly the right call for a first-time visit.)

sections.title: GO Fest 2026 — the complete guide
sections.body:
Pokémon GO Fest Copenhagen runs at Fælledparken, Jun 12–14, 2026, in partnership with the LEGO Group. This is the largest live Pokémon GO event in Europe in 2026.

Tickets — buy at pokemongo.com/en/gofest/copenhagen/tickets:

  • Park Experience ticket: 200 kr (≈$31) per session. Choose Morning (9:00–13:00) or Afternoon (14:00–18:00). Includes one park session plus full citywide access that day.
  • City Explorer Pass: 135 kr — citywide gameplay only, no park entry; available Jun 11–14.
  • Tickets are non-refundable and first-come-first-served.

Optional add-ons (purchased separately, any session):

  • Premier Access: 640 kr (≈$99) — exclusive physical collectible + voucher codes for 100+ in-game items
  • Raid Lover: 112 kr — up to 18 daily Raid Passes from spinning Gym Photo Discs + bonus raid XP and candy
  • Egg-thusiast: 112 kr — ¼ egg hatch distance + tripled hatch rewards for the day

LEGO Stamp Rally — opens Friday Jun 12 (first day of GO Fest):

  • LEGO Store Vimmelskaftet 37: Fri–Sat 10:00–18:00 · Sun 11:00–17:00
  • LEGO Store Tivoli Gardens: Fri–Sun 10:00–22:00

Spin PokéStops at each store for exclusive GO Fest stickers and Timed Research. Collect 5 stamps across both stores to unlock a Pikachu with Special Background encounter reward. The Tivoli Gardens store has significantly longer hours — use it for evening stamp collection. Official LEGO announcement: pokemongo.com/news/gofest-2026-copenhagen-lego.

Pack for a long warm day: battery bank (essential — GO Fest drains phones fast), water, sunscreen, a hat, and comfortable shoes. You'll be standing and walking for hours.

sections.title: What to eat in Copenhagen — a first-timer's guide
sections.body:
Copenhagen has one of the world's best food cultures at every price point, from two-euro bakery buns to multi-course tasting menus. Here's what to prioritise on this particular trip.

Danish classics to try at least once:

  • Smørrebrød — Denmark's national lunch: an open-faced sandwich on dense dark rye bread (rugbrød). Classic toppings include pickled herring, roast beef, egg and remoulade, or fresh shrimp. This is a midday meal, not dinner. ≈80–160 DKK at a proper smørrebrød restaurant.
  • Wienerbrød — Danish pastry. Flaky, laminated, and far better here than any 'Danish' you've had anywhere else. ≈20–45 DKK at a decent bakery.
  • Kanelsnegl or Kardemommesnurrer — cinnamon or cardamom bun. The current must-order pastry in the city. ≈25–40 DKK.
  • Pølse — Danish hot dog from a red pølsevogn cart. Red sausage in a soft bun, topped with remoulade, crispy onions, and pickles. ≈30–50 DKK. One of the most authentic things you can eat on the street here.
  • Stegt flæsk med persillesovs — crispy pork belly strips with parsley sauce, considered Denmark's national dish. Found at traditional lunch restaurants.

Seafood: Cold Nordic shrimp (rejer) piled on buttered rugbrød with mayonnaise and lemon is exceptional and uniquely Danish. Fresh smoked fish is worth seeking at Torvehallerne's fish counters.

New Nordic: Copenhagen essentially invented this global restaurant movement — seasonal, foraged, and fermented. At the budget end, Folkehuset Absalon does a communal long-table dinner for 75–100 DKK (see restaurant picks). At the high end, Alchemist runs 50+ micro-courses for ≈3,000–5,000 DKK per person and books months ahead.

Street food: Reffen (Tue Jun 9) — 60+ stalls open from 11:30 daily, perfect after Kastellet.

sections.title: Where to eat — picks near your route
sections.body:
Ordered by proximity to CABINN Nørreport and the Jun 11–13 daily routes. All DKK prices are approximate — verify before going.

Steps from the hotel (Nørreport):

  • Torvehallerne, Frederiksborggade 21 — Copenhagen's best covered food market, directly outside Nørreport station. 60+ stalls, all price points. Standouts: the fresh fish counter, smørrebrød stalls, The Coffee Collective, pastries. Hours: Mon–Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 10:00–18:00 (closed Sun some stalls vary — arrive by 17:30 to be safe). ≈80–160 DKK for a proper lunch. The single most convenient food stop on this entire itinerary.

On the Strøget and Thursday route:

  • Selma, Rømersgade 20 (Indre By) — Michelin Bib Gourmand, modern smørrebrød at lunch and tasting menu in the evening. Herring with nettles, roast beef with pickled things. ≈150–250 DKK per person for lunch.
  • Conditori La Glace, Skoubogade 3 (just off Strøget) — Denmark's oldest confectionery, founded 1870. Layer cakes and pastries that are genuinely hard to find elsewhere. ≈40–90 DKK for a slice and coffee.

Bakeries — open daily, everywhere:

  • Hart Bageri (multiple: Gammel Kongevej, Refshaleøen/Reffen area) — widely rated Copenhagen's best sourdough. ≈25–55 DKK.
  • Juno the Bakery, Elmegade 10 (near Fælledparken — good for a pastry before Friday's GO Fest walk) — famous cardamom buns. ≈30–50 DKK. ≈ Confirm address before going.
  • Lagkagehuset (multiple city-centre locations) — reliable chain for pastries, smørrebrød to go, coffee. ≈30–60 DKK.

Budget and local:

  • Folkehuset Absalon, Sønder Blvd 73 (Vesterbro) — communal long-table Danish dinner served at 18:00 daily. 75 DKK Sun–Wed, 100 DKK Thu–Sat. Book a ticket online by 16:00 on the day, or try the door from 17:00. One of the city's best bargains and genuinely social — not a tourist trap.
  • Doomsday Deli, Gulbergsgade (Nørrebro, ~20 min from CABINN) — thick, layered sandwiches with a strong local following. ≈80–120 DKK.

Already on the itinerary: Vækst — Sat Jun 13 at 17:30. Reservation confirmed.

⚠️ Confirm all addresses via Google Maps before you go. Restaurant details change.

sections.title: Shopping in Copenhagen — where to go and what to look for
sections.body:
Strøget is the 1.1 km pedestrian spine from Rådhuspladsen to Kongens Nytorv — the longest pedestrian shopping street in Europe. The middle section is mostly international chains (H&M, Zara, the LEGO flagship at Vimmelskaftet 37). The ends reward attention: the Rådhuspladsen end has more Danish names; the Kongens Nytorv end has Illum (Østergade 52 — seven floors, designer-tier) and Magasin du Nord (Kongens Nytorv 13 — more accessible, with an excellent basement food hall). Side streets off Strøget — especially Købmagergade and Kronprinsensgade — carry independent boutiques and jewellers.

By neighbourhood:

  • Nørrebro (Jægersborggade, Ravnsborggade): independent boutiques, vintage stores, vinyl record shops, local designers. About 20 minutes' walk from CABINN. The best neighbourhood for non-chain shopping in Copenhagen.
  • Vesterbro (Istedgade, Værnedamsvej): vintage clothes, independent cafes, concept stores near the old meatpacking district (Kødbyen). Slightly grittier, more neighbourhood feel.
  • Torvehallerne: the best source of edible souvenirs — Danish honey, rye crackers, smoked fish, craft beer, Lakrids liquorice by Johan Bülow.

By price tier:

  • Budget: Flying Tiger Copenhagen and Søstrene Grene — both Danish originals, both cheap, fun and genuinely worth a visit. Both on or near Strøget.
  • Mid-range: Rains (rainwear — good VAT-refund item), Samsøe Samsøe, Ecco
  • Higher-end: Hay (design and homewares), Royal Copenhagen (porcelain, Amagertorv 6, on Strøget), Georg Jensen (silver, same street), Pandora

VAT refund: Denmark charges 25% VAT. Non-EU visitors can claim a refund on purchases of 300 DKK or more at participating stores. Ask for a Global Blue or Planet tax-free form when you buy, then process it at the airport before departure. Rains gear, for example, typically costs around $60–65 after the refund versus $90–100 in the US (≈ figures approximate — verify at point of purchase).

sections.title: Souvenir ideas — Denmark
items: A Royal Copenhagen piece — even a small ornament (Amagertorv 6, on Strøget)
Something from Flying Tiger Copenhagen (a Danish original that most visitors haven't heard of)
A LEGO store-exclusive set — check for GO Fest weekend exclusives
A Dannebrog (Danish flag) or Copenhagen enamel pin
Lakrids by Johan Bülow — premium Danish liquorice available at Torvehallerne
Danish craft beer from Torvehallerne — Mikkeller is the famous Copenhagen name

sections.title: Collector's checklist — pins, mugs & merch
sections.body: For souvenir hunters: a Starbucks 'Been There' Copenhagen or Denmark mug. There's a Hard Rock Cafe in Copenhagen and another in Oslo (Karl Johans gate 45) — but none in Malmö; the nearest is Stockholm. The LEGO flagship at Vimmelskaftet 37 stocks store-exclusive sets and GO Fest weekend activations run Friday through Sunday — you have three days of opportunities to visit.

sections.title: A day across the bridge — Malmö, Sweden

items.name: Malmö, Sweden
items.body: Cobbled medieval squares (Gamla Staden, Lilla Torg) and the twisting Turning Torso skyscraper on the waterfront. The Øresund Bridge crossing is an experience in itself. Buy a cross-border Øresundståg ticket at Nørreport — City Pass stops at the border. Bring your passport.

sections.title: Souvenir ideas — Sweden
items: A Dala horse (the classic Swedish souvenir)
Swedish pick-and-mix candy — lösgodis is a national institution
A Malmö or Sweden enamel pin

sections.title: Overnight to Oslo — how the ferry works

items.name: Oslo, Norway
items.body: The Go Nordic Cruiseline overnight ferry departs Dampfærgevej 30 at 16:30 (check in by 16:15), sails overnight in a cabin, and docks at Vippetangen in Oslo around 10:00. That gives you roughly five hours ashore: the marble Opera House roof, Aker Brygge waterfront, and Akershus Fortress. Reboard by 15:30. No phone signal mid-sea — download everything before you board.

img.file: Oslo Opera House seen from Langkaia.JPG

sections.title: Souvenir ideas — Norway
items: A Norwegian wool sweater (Oleana or Dale of Norway are classic brands)
Brunost (brown cheese) and a traditional cheese slicer
Freia chocolate — Norway's most iconic confectionery brand

sections.title: Sources & links — everything verified for this guide
sections.body:
Every fact in this guide has a source. Click any link below to verify it yourself or check for updates.

GO Fest 2026

  • GO Fest 2026 Copenhagen — official tickets & add-on prices · Park ticket 200 kr, Premier Access 640 kr, Raid Lover 112 kr, Egg-thusiast 112 kr
  • GO Fest × LEGO — official announcement · LEGO store hours, Stamp Rally details

Transport & transit

  • DOT City Pass — official pricing page · 24h 100 DKK / 48h 160 DKK / 96h 280 DKK / 120h 340 DKK (Small, zones 1–4 incl. airport). No 72h option exists.
  • Rejseplanen · Official Copenhagen transit journey planner
  • Go Nordic Cruiseline · Copenhagen–Oslo overnight ferry (formerly DFDS route, operator changed Nov 2024). Departs 16:30 Copenhagen / arrives 10:00 Oslo. Terminal: Dampfærgevej 30.
  • Copenhagen Card — verified prices · 589 DKK / 24h · 859 DKK / 48h · 1,039 DKK / 72h (all zones + 80+ attractions free)

Attractions

  • Tivoli Gardens — ticket booking · Dynamic (date-based) pricing from ≈150 DKK in summer. Buy in advance to skip the queue.
  • Grundtvig's Church — opening hours · Closed Mondays. Open Tue–Sat 10:00–15:45, Sun 12:30–15:45. Plan Thursday visit accordingly.
  • Reffen street food market · Food stalls open 11:30–21:30 daily

Food & markets

  • Torvehallerne food market · Mon–Sat 10:00–18:00, Sun 10:00–18:00 (some stalls vary)
  • Folkehuset Absalon — communal dinner · Sønder Blvd 73. 75 DKK Sun–Wed, 100 DKK Thu–Sat. Served at 18:00 daily; book online by 16:00.
  • Selma — Michelin Bib Gourmand · Rømersgade 20. Modern smørrebrød at lunch.

Tours & activities

  • GetYourGuide — Copenhagen canal tours · Stromma classic canal tour ≈129 DKK / Netto-Bådene budget option ≈60 DKK per adult
  • RED Sightseeing (Stromma) — hop-on-hop-off bus · 24h ≈199–299 DKK per adult (verify before booking)

Health & safety

  • Steno Apotek · Vesterbrogade 6C, Copenhagen. Denmark's only 24-hour pharmacy. +45 33 14 82 66. Use night bell after 23:00.

Connectivity

  • Verizon TravelPass · $12/line/day in Denmark, Sweden and Norway. 5 GB high-speed per 24h window.
  • Verizon Unlimited Ultimate · 15 GB international roaming included; TravelPass cannot be added to this plan.

verified: ✓ Verified Jun 2026 — GO Fest prices, City Pass pricing, Copenhagen Card, ferry operator (Go Nordic Cruiseline), Torvehallerne hours, Folkehuset Absalon dinner prices all confirmed from primary sources. Re-check Tivoli dynamic pricing, GetYourGuide tour prices, and all restaurant details before you go.
